narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, predicting whether they'd pass on the first try based on academic and personal factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 36 (slightly under the 36.7 average), first-year school decile of 1 (far below the 5.71 average), third-year decile of 1 (well under the 5.53 average), and your plan to attend full-time like most students—and calculated an 82% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors driving this prediction, in order of influence, were your third-year grades in school, your first-year grades, and your LSAT score. Your low third-year decile ranking was the strongest push toward the failure prediction, as it signals to the model a significant drop or stagnation in performance compared to peers who typically climb higher. Similarly, your bottom-decile first-year grades reinforced this concern, standing out sharply against the middle-of-the-pack averages for successful students. On a more positive note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since it's close to the norm for bar passers, but it wasn't enough to offset the early academic weaknesses. Overall, your grades placed you in the lowest school deciles right from Year 1 and through Year 3, unlike typical admitted students who rank around the middle and show steady improvement leading to bar success. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority and your family income is solid, those didn't sway the outcome much here. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT if possible to boost it above average, addressing any undergraduate GPA gaps through a post-bac program or strong grad-level courses, and gathering letters explaining your early decile struggles—perhaps due to personal challenges—to show growth potential for your next application. Many students in similar positions rebound by targeting schools with holistic reviews or retaking key metrics, so persistence tailored to strengthening these core areas could turn this around.
